summary: Professor Kay-Tee Khaw discusses the symbiotic relationship between teaching and research, emphasizing that teaching critical evaluation skills is a researcher's true value. She also highlights the complexities of human epidemiological studies, noting the necessity of making sensible inferences from imperfect, real-world data and continuously monitoring changing environmental interactions.

principles.statement: Researchers provide the most value by teaching critical evaluation, not by providing right answers.
principles.rationale: Individuals need to find their own solutions and measure their own problems using critical and technical skills.
principles.source_quote: We don't teach right answers. We teach basic principles of how can we assess health impact, how can we evaluate critically the evidence because individuals have to find their own solutions.

principles.statement: Human studies require making sensible inferences from imperfect data.
principles.rationale: Unlike animal studies, human environments cannot be perfectly controlled and must be studied in real life.
principles.source_quote: Human studies are not like animal studies. You can’t control them perfectly. You have to study humans in a real life environment. The challenge is how you make sensible inferences from imperfect data.

principles.statement: Population health policies and interventions must be constantly evaluated and monitored.
principles.rationale: Environments and human interactions are constantly changing over time.
principles.source_quote: Environment and interactions are changing all the time, so that's why I think we need to evaluate and monitor constantly what we're doing.

mental_models.name: Health trajectory over the life course
mental_models.description: Viewing population health not as a static snapshot, but as a continuous path influenced by biological underpinnings, social determinants, and environmental factors over a person's entire life.
mental_models.example: Using technological developments to measure complex biological processes and pollution in large numbers of people to understand what determines their health trajectory.

signature_quotes.text: We don't teach right answers. We teach basic principles of how can we assess health impact, how can we evaluate critically the evidence because individuals have to find their own solutions.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the value of researchers as teachers.

signature_quotes.text: Human studies are not like animal studies. You can’t control them perfectly. You have to study humans in a real life environment. The challenge is how you make sensible inferences from imperfect data.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the fundamental challenge of human epidemiology.

signature_quotes.text: Environment and interactions are changing all the time, so that's why I think we need to evaluate and monitor constantly what we're doing.
signature_quotes.context: Advocating for ongoing population studies and policy assessments.

anti_patterns.description: Treating human studies like animal studies by expecting perfect control.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: You cannot perfectly control human subjects; they must be studied in real-life environments, which inherently produces imperfect data.
It deprives students of the critical and technical skills needed to evaluate evidence and find their own solutions to new problems.
